title: Glycan Graphs: The Network Behind Glycan Structures
text: Glycans naturally represented directed graphs. glyrepr, glycan structure stored outward-directed tree, vertex represents monosaccharide edge represents glycosidic linkage. Behind scenes, glycan_structure() object backed igraph object. workflows can stay glycan_structure() level, graph representation useful need custom structure analysis.
code: library(glyrepr)

title: Glycan Graphs: The Network Behind Glycan Structures
text: glycan can carry many kinds information: linear oriented C-atoms, basetype, substituents, configuration, anomeric center, ring size, linkage positions, . packages, Python’s glypy, store detailed glycan model. comprehensive strategy useful specialized tasks MS/MS spectra simulation, can overkill everyday omics research. glyrepr takes compact approach: feature can derived IUPAC-condensed text representation, glyrepr stores . Details configuration ring size stored directly, often predictable common carbohydrates needed many glycomics workflows. closer look IUPAC-condensed notation, see IUPAC-condensed vignette.

title: Glycan Graphs: The Network Behind Glycan Structures
text: pass glycan_structure() object directly igraph functions. First, extract underlying graph get_structure_graphs(): printed graph contains several pieces information. First line: Directed Named (“DN”) graph 5 vertices (sugar units) 4 edges (bonds). Graph-level attributes: anomer: anomeric configuration reducing end. Vertex attributes: name: unique ID monosaccharide. mono: monosaccharide type, “Hex” “HexNAc”. sub: chemical decorations attached monosaccharide. Edge attributes: linkage: monosaccharides connected, including bond positions configurations. Connection pattern: “1->2” means vertex 1 connects vertex 2. glyrepr treats bonds arrows pointing core toward branches. direction modeling choice makes traversal structure operations easier. can also plot graph igraph:
code: glycan <- n_glycan_core() graph <- get_structure_graphs(glycan) graph #> IGRAPH 9b09247 DN-- 5 4 --  #> + attr: anomer (g/c), name (v/c), mono (v/c), sub (v/c), linkage (e/c) #> + edges from 9b09247 (vertex names): #> [1] 3->1 3->2 4->3 5->4 plot(graph)

title: Glycan Graphs: The Network Behind Glycan Structures
text: vertex represents monosaccharide three key properties: Names: auto-generated identifiers, usually simple integers, anything long ’re unique: Monosaccharides: IUPAC-condensed names like “Hex”, “HexNAc”, “Glc”, “GlcNAc”. full list available monosaccharides, check SNFG notation run available_monosaccharides(). Substituents: Chemical decorations like “” (methyl), “Ac” (acetyl), “S” (sulfate), etc. Position matters. “3Me” = methyl position 3, “?S” = sulfate unknown position: Multiple decorations comma-separated sorted position:
code: igraph::V(graph)$name #> [1] "1" "2" "3" "4" "5" igraph::V(graph)$mono #> [1] "Man"    "Man"    "Man"    "GlcNAc" "GlcNAc" igraph::V(graph)$sub #> [1] "" "" "" "" "" glycan2 <- as_glycan_structure("Glc3Me6S(a1-") graph2 <- get_structure_graphs(glycan2) igraph::V(graph2)$sub #> [1] "3Me,6S"

title: Glycan Graphs: The Network Behind Glycan Structures
text: Edges represent glycosidic bonds simple powerful format: example “Gal” “” anomeric configuration, linking position 3 “GalNAc” position 1 “Gal”: glyrepr stores anomer information edges rather vertices. follows way linkages written IUPAC-condensed notation, example “Neu5Ac a2-3 linkage”.
code: <target anomeric config><target position> - <source position> glycan3 <- as_glycan_structure("Gal(a1-3)GalNAc(b1-") graph3 <- get_structure_graphs(glycan3) igraph::E(graph3)$linkage #> [1] "a1-3"

code: graph$anomer #> [1] "b1"

title: Glycan Graphs: The Network Behind Glycan Structures

title: Glycan Graphs: The Network Behind Glycan Structures
text: Working multiple glycans? use purrr: glycan structure vectors, glyrepr’s smap functions usually better fit: main advantage smap functions handle duplicates. Real datasets often contain many repeated structures, smap optimizes processing unique structures , efficiently expanding results back original dimensions. smap vignette covers workflow detail.
code: library(purrr)  glycans <- c(n_glycan_core(), o_glycan_core_1(), o_glycan_core_2()) graphs <- get_structure_graphs(glycans)  # Extract graphs first map_int(graphs, ~ igraph::vcount(.x))    # Then analyze #> [1] 5 2 3 smap_int(glycans, ~ igraph::vcount(.x))  # Direct analysis, no intermediate step. #> [1] 5 2 3

title: Getting Started with glyrepr

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: glycan composition vector can created either glycan_composition() as_glycan_composition(). glycan_composition() direct constructor. takes one named vectors, names monosaccharides values counts. creates glycan composition vector called comps three glycan compositions. as_glycan_composition() flexible can convert several input types. list named vectors: looks similar glycan_composition(), convenient compositions already stored list, example reading generating programmatically. character vector: compact notation ("H5N2") Byonic-style notation ("Hex(3)HexNAc(2)") supported. glycan structure vector, discuss :
code: comps <- glycan_composition(   c(Man = 5, GlcNAc = 2),   c(Man = 3, Gal = 2, GlcNAc = 4),   c(Man = 3, Gal = 2, GlcNAc = 4, Neu5Ac = 1, Fuc = 1) ) comps #> <glycan_composition[3]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) as_glycan_composition(list(   c(Man = 5, GlcNAc = 2),   c(Man = 3, Gal = 2, GlcNAc = 4),   c(Man = 3, Gal = 2, GlcNAc = 4, Neu5Ac = 1, Fuc = 1) )) #> <glycan_composition[3]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) comp_list <- list(   c(Man = 5, GlcNAc = 2),   c(Man = 3, Gal = 2, GlcNAc = 4),   c(Man = 3, Gal = 2, GlcNAc = 4, Neu5Ac = 1, Fuc = 1) ) as_glycan_composition(comp_list) #> <glycan_composition[3]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) as_glycan_composition(c("H5N2", "Hex(3)HexNAc(2)")) #> <glycan_composition[2]> #> [1] Hex(5)HexNAc(2) #> [2] Hex(3)HexNAc(2) strucs <- c(o_glycan_core_1(), o_glycan_core_2()) as_glycan_composition(strucs) #> <glycan_composition[2]> #> [1] Gal(1)GalNAc(1) #> [2] Gal(1)GlcNAc(1)GalNAc(1)

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: move , let’s briefly discuss two types monosaccharide names used glyrepr: Generic names: Hex, HexNAc, dHex, etc. Specific names: Man, GlcNAc, Fuc, etc. Generic names common mass spectrometry data, often difficult distinguish isomers MS evidence alone. example, one Hex residue Man, Gal, Glc, another hexose. Specific names carry biological detail common glycan databases literature. glyrepr supports types names, mix vector. rule applies glycan structure vectors.
code: # This raises an error because the monosaccharide names are mixed. try(as_glycan_composition(c("Hex(5)HexNAc(2)", "Man(5)GlcNAc(2)")), silent = TRUE)

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: main function inspecting glycan compositions count_mono(). Let’s demonstrate using comps vector created earlier. first argument glycan composition vector, second argument monosaccharide want count. count_mono() works generic specific monosaccharide names. understands relationship , can count level ask . Note “Man” “Gal” counted “Hex” . can also omit second argument get total monosaccharide count composition.
code: comps #> <glycan_composition[3]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) count_mono(comps, "Man") #> [1] 5 3 3 count_mono(comps, "Neu5Ac") #> [1] 0 0 1 count_mono(comps, "Hex") #> [1] 5 5 5 count_mono(comps) #> [1]  7  9 11

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: One useful mental model glycan composition vectors (glycan structure vectors) behave like atomic vectors. means support familiar operations like subsetting, concatenation, sorting. Concatenation: Subsetting: Length: Unique: Repeated: Sorting:
code: c(comps, comps) #> <glycan_composition[6]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) #> [4] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [5] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [6] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) comps[1:2] #> <glycan_composition[2]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) comps[integer()] #> <glycan_composition[0]> length(comps) #> [1] 3 dup_comps <- c(comps, comps) dup_comps #> <glycan_composition[6]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) #> [4] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [5] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [6] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) unique(dup_comps) #> <glycan_composition[3]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) rep(comps, times = 2) #> <glycan_composition[6]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) #> [4] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [5] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [6] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) sort(comps) #> <glycan_composition[3]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) sort(comps, decreasing = TRUE) #> <glycan_composition[3]> #> [1] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4) #> [3] Man(5)GlcNAc(2)

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: One useful features glycan composition vectors work smoothly tibbles data frames. can use tidyverse functions perform operations glycan composition column.
code: library(tibble)  tb <- tibble(   id = c("glycan1", "glycan2", "glycan3"),   composition = comps ) tb #> # A tibble: 3 × 2 #>   id      composition                          #>   <chr>   <comp>                               #> 1 glycan1 Man(5)GlcNAc(2)                      #> 2 glycan2 Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)                #> 3 glycan3 Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union  tb |>   mutate(n_sia = count_mono(composition, "Neu5Ac")) |>   filter(n_sia > 0) #> # A tibble: 1 × 3 #>   id      composition                          n_sia #>   <chr>   <comp>                               <int> #> 1 glycan3 Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(1)     1

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: Glycan composition vectors can also handle missing values. technical reasons, glycan composition vectors element names right now. practice, usually fine: composition remains data value, identifiers can live separate tibble column.
code: comps_with_na <- glycan_composition(c(Man = 5, GlcNAc = 2), NA) comps_with_na #> <glycan_composition[2]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] <NA> count_mono(comps_with_na, "Man") #> [1]  5 NA

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: Now let’s move core glycan representation: glycan structure vectors. Many ideas apply, including atomic vector nature, vectorized operations, seamless integration tibbles. structures complex compositions, additional features considerations keep mind.

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: glycan composition vectors, can create glycan structure vectors either glycan_structure() as_glycan_structure(). hood, glycan structures represented igraph objects, glycans naturally graph-like. time, need work graphs directly: glyrepr gives higher-level interface common structure operations. glycan_structure() direct constructor takes one igraph objects. Creating graphs manually can tedious, usually start as_glycan_structure() instead. can parse IUPAC-condensed strings glycan structure vectors. familiar IUPAC-condensed notation, check article quick introduction. recommend getting familiar notation, main text language communicating glycan structures glycoverse. Parsing IUPAC-condensed strings straightforward as_glycan_structure(). (parsing formats like GlycoCT, use glyparse package.)
code: strucs <- as_glycan_structure(c(   "Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-",   "Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-" )) strucs #> <glycan_structure[2]> #> [1] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1- #> [2] Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1- #> # Unique structures: 2

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: strucs prints bit like character vector colors, hood glycan structures stored igraph objects. can access underlying igraph objects using get_structure_graphs(). Details glycan structure modeled graph covered glycan graph vignette. need details everyday use, quick skim can make structure functions easier understand. count_mono() function also works glycan structure vectors. functions, including has_linkages(), get_mono_type(), get_structure_level(), inspect specific aspects structures.
code: get_structure_graphs(strucs) #> [[1]] #> IGRAPH 1395250 DN-- 2 1 --  #> + attr: anomer (g/c), name (v/c), mono (v/c), sub (v/c), linkage (e/c) #> + edge from 1395250 (vertex names): #> [1] 2->1 #>  #> [[2]] #> IGRAPH 534d29e DN-- 3 2 --  #> + attr: anomer (g/c), name (v/c), mono (v/c), sub (v/c), linkage (e/c) #> + edges from 534d29e (vertex names): #> [1] 3->1 3->2 count_mono(strucs, "Gal") #> [1] 1 1 # This function works element-wise has_linkages(strucs) #> [1] TRUE TRUE get_mono_type(strucs) #> [1] "concrete" get_structure_level(strucs) #> [1] "intact"

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: structures seen far “intact” structures. contain specific monosaccharides complete linkage information. real datasets, though, glycan structures often missing information. example, might know topology linkages, might know generic monosaccharide classes. accommodate scenarios, glyrepr defines four levels glycan structures: Intact: specific monosaccharides complete linkage information. Partial: specific monosaccharides, least one missing linkage annotation. Topological: specific monosaccharides, linkage information missing. Basic: generic monosaccharides, linkage information treated missing. Structure levels defined vector level, one glycan structure vector one level. Let’s see examples. Intact structures: Partial structures: Topological structures: Basic structures: theory, can something like "Hex(b1-3)HexNAc(a1-", generic monosaccharides linkages intact. practice, linkage information usually harder obtain monosaccharide identities, situation rare. create vector, glyrepr classifies "basic" warns .
code: as_glycan_structure(c(   "Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-",   "Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-" )) |> get_structure_level() #> [1] "intact" as_glycan_structure(c(   "Gal(b1-?)GalNAc(a1-",   "Gal(b1-?)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-" )) |> get_structure_level() #> [1] "partial" as_glycan_structure(c(   "Gal(??-?)GalNAc(??-",   "Gal(??-?)[GlcNAc(??-?)]GalNAc(??-" )) |> get_structure_level() #> [1] "topological" as_glycan_structure(c(   "Hex(??-?)HexNAc(??-",   "Hex(??-?)[HexNAc(??-?)]HexNAc(??-" )) |> get_structure_level() #> [1] "basic" as_glycan_structure(c(   "Hex(a1-3)HexNAc(a1-",   "Hex(a1-3)[HexNAc(b1-6)]HexNAc(a1-" )) |> get_structure_level() #> Warning: Generic glycan structures with linkage annotations are treated as "basic". #> ℹ Linkage information is ignored when residues are generic. #> [1] "basic"

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: Glycan structure vectors also support vectorized operations like subsetting concatenation. also structure-specific helpers reducing resolution, removing linkages, removing substituents.
code: strucs #> <glycan_structure[2]> #> [1] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1- #> [2] Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1- #> # Unique structures: 2 reduce_structure_level(strucs, to_level = "basic") #> <glycan_structure[2]> #> [1] Hex(??-?)HexNAc(??- #> [2] HexNAc(??-?)[Hex(??-?)]HexNAc(??- #> # Unique structures: 2 remove_linkages(strucs)  # same as reduce_structure_level(strucs, to_level = "topological") #> <glycan_structure[2]> #> [1] Gal(??-?)GalNAc(??- #> [2] GlcNAc(??-?)[Gal(??-?)]GalNAc(??- #> # Unique structures: 2 strucs_with_subs <- as_glycan_structure(c(   "Gal6S(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-",   "Gal6S(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-" )) remove_substituents(strucs_with_subs) #> <glycan_structure[2]> #> [1] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1- #> [2] Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1- #> # Unique structures: 2

title: Getting Started with glyrepr
text: Like glycan composition vectors, glycan structure vectors can also handle missing values. Different glycan composition vectors, though, glycan structure vectors can names. useful feature introduced version 0.10.0. find names useful working glymotif package.
code: strings <- c(   glycan1 = "Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-",   glycan2 = "Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-" ) as_glycan_structure(strings) #> <glycan_structure[2]> #> [1] glycan1  Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1- #> [2] glycan2  Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1- #> # Unique structures: 2

what: Why Are There So Many Glycan Formats?
title: IUPAC-Condensed Glycan Notation
text: Different communities developed different ways describe glycans, optimized specific use case. formats easy people read, better databases software, others preserve detailed chemical information. Let’s use one N-glycan example:  molecule can written several formats: IUPAC-condensed: IUPAC-extended: WURCS: InChI: format serves purpose: IUPAC formats: readable text formats describing glycan structures. WURCS/GlycoCT: structured formats designed databases software. Semantic formats: formats designed linking data across platforms. Chemical formats: formats used detailed chemical representation.

title: IUPAC-Condensed Glycan Notation
text: building glyrepr, needed native text format glycan structures. IUPAC-condensed practical choice balances readability information content. Human-readable: structures can usually interpreted without specialized software. Information-rich: captures monosaccharides, branches, linkages, anomers, substituents needed many glycomics analyses. Widely used: familiar many researchers glycomics community. Flexible: can represent simple complex glycan structures. formats WURCS GlycoCT, broader glycoverse workflow can use parser packages glyparse.

title: IUPAC-Condensed Glycan Notation
text: Every glycan built monosaccharide units, IUPAC notation gives one short abbreviation: broader list symbols, see SNFG website run available_monosaccharides().

title: IUPAC-Condensed Glycan Notation
text: Glycans can contain chemical modifications called substituents. IUPAC-condensed notation, substituents written monosaccharide name. Neu5Ac9Ac = sialic acid extra acetyl group position 9. Glc3Me = glucose methyl group position 3. GlcNAc6Ac = N-acetylglucosamine acetylation position 6. Format rule: Position number + Modification type Example: 6Ac = “acetyl group position 6”

title: Efficient Glycan Manipulation with smap
text: glyrepr implements optimization called unique structure storage. Instead storing thousands identical graphs, stores unique ones keeps track original positions belong . Let’s see action: Notice object reports 5 unique structures. vector 5,000 elements, 5 unique graphs stored internally. can verify directly:
code: # Our test data: some common glycan structures iupacs <- c(   "Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-",  # N-glycan core   "Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-",                                    # O-glycan core 1   "Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1-",                     # O-glycan core 2   "Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(a1-",          # Branched mannose   "GlcNAc6Ac(b1-4)Glc3Me(a1-"                              # With decorations )  struc <- as_glycan_structure(iupacs)  # Now create a realistic dataset with lots of repetition. large_struc <- rep(struc, 1000)  # 5,000 total structures large_struc #> <glycan_structure[5000]> #> [1] Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1- #> [2] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1- #> [3] Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1- #> [4] Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(a1- #> [5] GlcNAc6Ac(b1-4)Glc3Me(a1- #> [6] Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1- #> [7] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1- #> [8] Gal(b1-3)[GlcNAc(b1-6)]GalNAc(a1- #> [9] Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(a1- #> [10] GlcNAc6Ac(b1-4)Glc3Me(a1- #> ... (4990 more not shown) #> # Unique structures: 5 # Only 5 unique graphs are stored internally length(attr(large_struc, "structures")) #> [1] 0  # But we have 5,000 total elements length(large_struc) #> [1] 5000

title: Efficient Glycan Manipulation with smap
text: Quick examples extended family: Performance note: simap functions benefit unique structure optimization. Since element different index, combination (structure, index) always unique, breaking deduplication makes smap functions fast. Use simap truly need position information.
code: # smap2: Apply function with additional parameters thresholds <- c(3, 4, 5) large_enough <- smap2_lgl(struc[1:3], thresholds, function(g, threshold) {   igraph::vcount(g) >= threshold }) large_enough #> [1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE # simap: Include position information indexed_report <- simap_chr(large_struc[1:3], function(g, i) {   paste0("#", i, ": ", igraph::vcount(g), " vertices") }) indexed_report #> [1] "#1: 5 vertices" "#2: 2 vertices" "#3: 3 vertices"

title: Efficient Glycan Manipulation with smap
text: main performance benefit smap functions comes automatic deduplication: higher redundancy, larger performance gain. real glycoproteomics datasets repeated structures, optimization can provide 10x speedups.
code:
# Create a large dataset with high redundancy huge_struc <- rep(struc, 5000)  # 25,000 structures, only 5 unique  cat("Dataset size:", length(huge_struc), "structures
") #> Dataset size: 25000 structures cat("Unique structures:", length(attr(huge_struc, "structures")), "
") #> Unique structures: 0 cat("Redundancy factor:", length(huge_struc) / length(attr(huge_struc, "structures")), "x
") #> Redundancy factor: Inf x  library(tictoc)  # Optimized approach: smap only processes 5 unique structures tic("smap_int (optimized)") vertex_counts_optimized <- smap_int(huge_struc, igraph::vcount) toc() #> smap_int (optimized): 0.003 sec elapsed  # Naive approach: extract all graphs and process each one tic("Naive approach (all graphs)") all_graphs <- get_structure_graphs(huge_struc)  # Extracts all 25,000 graphs vertex_counts_naive <- purrr::map_int(all_graphs, igraph::vcount) toc() #> Naive approach (all graphs): 0.197 sec elapsed  # Verify results are equivalent (though data types may differ) all.equal(vertex_counts_optimized, vertex_counts_naive) #> [1] TRUE

code: library(glyrepr)  # Create glycan compositions glycan_composition(   c(Man = 5, GlcNAc = 2),   c(Man = 3, Gal = 2, GlcNAc = 4, Fuc = 1, Neu5Ac = 2) ) #> <glycan_composition[2]> #> [1] Man(5)GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(3)Gal(2)GlcNAc(4)Fuc(1)Neu5Ac(2)  # Parse IUPAC-condensed glycan text representation # `glyrepr` supports IUPAC-condensed glycan text representation natively. # For other formats like WURCS or glycoCT, use the `glyparse` package. # For example, the following two glycan structures are equivalent: structures <- as_glycan_structure(c("GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(?1-", "Man(a1-2)GlcNAc(?1-"))  # Get the composition of a glycan structure as_glycan_composition(structures) #> <glycan_composition[2]> #> [1] GlcNAc(2) #> [2] Man(1)GlcNAc(1)  # Count the number of given residues count_mono(structures, "Hex") #> [1] 0 1 count_mono(glycan_composition(c(Man = 3, GlcNAc = 2, Gal = 2)), "Hex") #> [1] 5

title: Convert to Glycan Composition — as_glycan_composition
text: function uses vctrs casting framework type conversion. converting glycan structures, monosaccharides substituents counted. Substituents extracted sub attribute vertex structure. example, vertex sub = "3Me" contributes one "" substituent composition. Simple composition strings use one-letter residue codes: "H" "Hex", "N" "HexNAc", "F" "dHex", "S"/"" "NeuAc", "G" "NeuGc". what: Get the Structure Resolution Levels — get_structure_level
title: Get the Structure Resolution Levels — get_structure_level
text: Glycan structures can four possible levels resolution: "intact": monosaccharides concrete (e.g. "Man", "GlcNAc"), linkage anomer contains "?". "partial": monosaccharides concrete (e.g. "Man", "GlcNAc"), least one linkage anomer contains "?", least one linkage anomer non-"?" annotation. "topological": monosaccharides concrete (e.g. "Man", "GlcNAc"), linkages anomers completely unknown ("??-?"/"??"). "basic": monosaccharides generic (e.g. "Hex", "HexNAc"). Note theory can glycan generic monosaccharides linkages determined. example, "Hex(b1-3)HexNAc(a1-" valid glycan structure. reality, almost impossible, linkage information far difficult acquire monosaccharide information. kind glycan structure also assigned "basic" level.

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
text: glycan structure vector vctrs record additional S3 class glyrepr_structure. Therefore, sloop::s3_class() returns class hierarchy c("glyrepr_structure", "vctrs_rcrd"). glycan structure must satisfy following constraints:

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
text: Must directed graph outward tree structure (reducing end root) Must graph attribute anomer format "a1" "b1" Unknown parts can represented "?", e.g., "?1", "?", "??"

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
text: mono: Monosaccharide names, must known monosaccharide types Generic names: Hex, HexNAc, dHex, NeuAc, etc. Concrete names: Glc, Gal, Man, GlcNAc, etc. mix generic concrete names NA values allowed sub: Substituent information Single substituent format: "xY" (x = position, Y = substituent name), e.g., "2Ac", "3S" Multiple substituents separated commas ordered position, e.g., "3Me,4Ac", "2S,6P" substituents represented empty string ""

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
text: linkage: Glycosidic linkage information format "/bX-Y" Standard format: e.g., "b1-4", "a2-3" Unknown positions allowed: "a1-?", "b?-3", "??-?" Partially unknown positions: "a1-3/6", "a1-3/6/9" NA values allowed

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
text: indices vertices linkages glycan correspond directly order IUPAC-condensed string, printed print glycan_structure(). example, glycan Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-, vertices "Man", "Man", "Man", "GlcNAc", "GlcNAc", linkages "a1-3", "a1-6", "b1-4", "b1-4".

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
text: Glycan structure vectors support NA values representing missing unknown structures: Create glycan_structure(NA) glycan_structure(NULL) Combine valid structures: c(struct1, NA, struct2) Convert character: as_glycan_structure(c("Glc(a1-", NA)) smap functions skip NA elements gracefully .na() returns TRUE NA elements

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
text: Glycan structure vectors can names, preserved operations. particularly useful working glymotif package.

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
text: One side-effect current implementation can treat glycan structure vector pure character vector IUPAC-condensed strings. fact, .character() returns TRUE glycan structure vector, stringr functions work directly vector. However, still recommend using .character() explicitly convert character needed, avoid confusion ensure intended behavior clear.

title: Create a Glycan Structure Vector — glycan_structure
code: library(igraph)  # Example 1: Create a simple glycan structure GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc graph <- make_graph(~ 1-+2)  # Create graph with two monosaccharides V(graph)$mono <- c("GlcNAc", "GlcNAc")  # Set monosaccharide types V(graph)$sub <- ""  # No substituents E(graph)$linkage <- "b1-4"  # b1-4 glycosidic linkage graph$anomer <- "a1"  # a anomeric carbon  # Create glycan structure vector simple_struct <- glycan_structure(graph) print(simple_struct) #> <glycan_structure[1]> #> [1] GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(a1- #> # Unique structures: 1  # Example 2: Use predefined glycan core structures n_core <- n_glycan_core()  # N-glycan core structure o_core1 <- o_glycan_core_1()  # O-glycan Core 1 structure  # Example 3: Create complex structure with substituents complex_graph <- make_graph(~ 1-+2-+3) V(complex_graph)$mono <- c("GlcNAc", "Gal", "Neu5Ac") V(complex_graph)$sub <- c("", "", "")  # Add substituents as needed E(complex_graph)$linkage <- c("b1-4", "a2-3") complex_graph$anomer <- "b1"  complex_struct <- glycan_structure(complex_graph) print(complex_struct) #> <glycan_structure[1]> #> [1] Neu5Ac(a2-3)Gal(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1- #> # Unique structures: 1  # Example 4: Check if object is a glycan structure is_glycan_structure(simple_struct)  # TRUE #> [1] TRUE is_glycan_structure(graph)          # FALSE #> [1] FALSE

title: Generate Possible Linkages — possible_linkages
text: Given obscure linkage format ("?", e.g. "a2-?"), function generates possible linkages based format. See valid_linkages() details. ranges possible anomers, first positions, second positions can specified using anomer_range, pos1_range, pos2_range.

title: Generate Possible Linkages — possible_linkages

title: Generate Possible Linkages — possible_linkages
text: linkage linkage string. anomer_range character vector possible anomers. Default c("", "b"). pos1_range numeric vector possible first positions. Default 1:2. pos2_range numeric vector possible second positions. Default 1:9. include_unknown logical value. TRUE, "?" included. Default FALSE.

title: Generate Possible Linkages — possible_linkages
text: character vector possible linkages.

title: Generate Possible Linkages — possible_linkages
code: possible_linkages("a2-?") #> [1] "a2-1" "a2-2" "a2-3" "a2-4" "a2-5" "a2-6" "a2-7" "a2-8" "a2-9" possible_linkages("??-2") #> [1] "a1-2" "b1-2" "a2-2" "b2-2" possible_linkages("a1-3") #> [1] "a1-3" possible_linkages("a?-?", pos1_range = 2, pos2_range = c(2, 3)) #> [1] "a2-2" "a2-3" possible_linkages("?1-6", include_unknown = TRUE) #> [1] "a1-6" "b1-6" "?1-6"

code: # Create a structure vector with duplicates core1 <- o_glycan_core_1() core2 <- n_glycan_core() structures <- c(core1, core2, core1)  # core1 appears twice  # Map a function that counts vertices - only computed twice, not three times smap_int(structures, igraph::vcount) #> [1] 2 5 2  # Map a function that returns logical smap_lgl(structures, function(g) igraph::vcount(g) > 5) #> [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE  # Use purrr-style lambda functions smap_int(structures, ~ igraph::vcount(.x)) #> [1] 2 5 2 smap_lgl(structures, ~ igraph::vcount(.x) > 5) #> [1] FALSE FALSE FALSE  # Map a function that modifies structure (must return igraph) add_vertex_names <- function(g) {   if (!("name" %in% igraph::vertex_attr_names(g))) {     igraph::set_vertex_attr(g, "name", value = paste0("v", seq_len(igraph::vcount(g))))   } else {     g   } } smap_structure(structures, add_vertex_names) #> <glycan_structure[3]> #> [1] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1- #> [2] Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1- #> [3] Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1- #> # Unique structures: 2

text: Linkages compared lexicographically: First anomeric configuration: ? > b > first position: ? > numbers (numerically) Finally second position: ? > numbers (numerically) Smaller linkages placed backbone, larger ones branches.

title: Convert Glycan Structure to IUPAC-like Sequence — structure_to_iupac
code: # Simple linear structure structure_to_iupac(o_glycan_core_1()) #> [1] "Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-"  # Branched structure structure_to_iupac(n_glycan_core()) #> [1] "Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"  # Structure with substituents graph <- igraph::make_graph(~ 1-+2) igraph::V(graph)$mono <- c("Glc", "GlcNAc") igraph::V(graph)$sub <- c("3Me", "6Ac") igraph::E(graph)$linkage <- "b1-4" graph$anomer <- "a1" glycan <- glycan_structure(graph) structure_to_iupac(glycan)  # Returns "GlcNAc6Ac(b1-4)Glc3Me(a1-" #> [1] "GlcNAc6Ac(b1-4)Glc3Me(a1-"  # Vectorized structures structs <- c(o_glycan_core_1(), n_glycan_core()) structure_to_iupac(structs) #> [1] "Gal(b1-3)GalNAc(a1-"                                 #> [2] "Man(a1-3)[Man(a1-6)]Man(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-4)GlcNAc(b1-"

title: Check if Linkages are Valid — valid_linkages
text: Valid linkages form "a1-2", "b1-4", "?-1", etc. Specifically, pattern xy-z: x: anomer, either "", "b", "?". y: first position, either "1", "2" "?". z: second position, either 1-9 digit "?". Can also multiple positions separated "/", e.g. "1/2/3". "?" used "/".
